statement: On 20 October 2012, Iceland held a six-question advisory referendum on constitutional reform proposals drafted by the Stjórnlagaráð (Constitutional Council). The first and most significant question — 'Do you wish the proposals of the Constitutional Council to form the basis of a new Constitution?' — received 66.9% yes votes (49,486 yes vs 24,521 no). All six questions received majority yes votes. Turnout was 48.9% (115,890 of approximately 237,000 eligible voters), significantly lower than typical parliamentary election turnout of 80–85%. The referendum was advisory (ráðgefandi), not legally binding, as the Icelandic constitution does not provide for binding referendums on policy questions. Despite the clear majority in favour, no subsequent Alþingi has enacted the draft into law.
source_name: Landskjörstjórn (National Electoral Commission) — Official results of the 20 October 2012 referendum
caveats: The advisory nature of the referendum means Alþingi was under no legal obligation to act on the results. Critics of inaction argue the democratic mandate was clear; defenders of inaction cite the low turnout and argue that constitutional change requires broader consensus. The six questions were also criticised for being leading or bundled.

statement: The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a natural resources clause (auðlindaákvæðið) stating: 'Iceland's natural resources which are not in private ownership are the common and perpetual property of the nation. No one may acquire the natural resources or their attached rights for ownership or permanent use, and they may never be sold or mortgaged.' The clause envisions a licensing regime with leases limited to 'a modest period of time.' This provision was the subject of Question 2 in the 2012 referendum ('Do you wish that a new Constitution include a provision on national ownership of natural resources, as is in the draft?'), which received 82.9% yes — the highest approval of any of the six questions. The provision is considered economically significant as Iceland's key export sectors (fishing, energy-intensive industry, tourism) rely on natural resources.
source_name: Stjórnlagaráð — A Proposal for a new Constitution for the Republic of Iceland (English translation)
caveats: The clause is controversial. Supporters argue it prevents privatisation of fishing quotas and energy rights. Opponents, particularly in the fisheries sector, argue it could undermine the existing quota system and create legal uncertainty for existing resource-use agreements. The clause's interaction with EU common fisheries policy would be a key issue in any accession negotiations.

statement: The Constitutional Council's 2011 draft constitution includes a provision stating that compulsory military service may never be introduced into law. This strengthens the existing constitutional position: Article 75 of the current 1944 Constitution permits conscription in cases of 'national danger', though it has never been exercised in practice. Iceland has no standing military and has never had compulsory military service. The draft provision would make the prohibition absolute, removing even the theoretical possibility. This clause has gained renewed relevance in the EU membership debate, as opponents argue that evolving EU defence integration (PESCO, European Defence Fund, Strategic Compass) could eventually lead to pressure for military contributions from all member states, though the EU has no mechanism to impose conscription on member states.
source_name: Stjórnlagaráð — A Proposal for a new Constitution for the Republic of Iceland (English translation); Constitute Project — Iceland 2011D
caveats: EU membership does not require military conscription — Ireland, Austria, and Malta are EU members with varying forms of military neutrality. CSDP participation is voluntary. However, the EU's defence ambitions are evolving rapidly post-Ukraine, and future developments are uncertain. The draft constitution's prohibition is not yet in force as the draft has not been adopted.

statement: Following the 2012 advisory referendum in which 66.9% supported using the Constitutional Council's draft as the basis for a new constitution, successive Alþingi sessions have failed to enact constitutional reform. The centre-right government of Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson and Bjarni Benediktsson (2013–2016) effectively shelved the draft, arguing it needed further expert review. Subsequent governments (2016–2017, 2017–2021, 2021–2024) introduced partial constitutional bills — notably on natural resources and electoral reform — but none passed into law. The Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society) and other civil society organisations have campaigned to honour the referendum result, framing parliamentary inaction as a democratic deficit. As of early 2026, no provisions of the 2011 draft have been enacted, making it one of the most protracted constitutional reform efforts in modern European democracies.
source_name: Stjórnarskrárfélagið (Constitutional Society of Iceland); Alþingi parliamentary records; Thorvaldur Gylfason, 'Democracy on Ice' (openDemocracy, 2016)
caveats: Whether parliament was obligated to act on an advisory referendum is a matter of political philosophy, not law. Some constitutional scholars argue the draft had technical deficiencies requiring revision. The low turnout (48.9%) weakens the democratic mandate argument, though a two-thirds majority among those who voted is substantial. The issue remains politically divisive.

statement: The United States and Iceland signed a bilateral defence agreement on 5 May 1951, under which the US accepted responsibility for Iceland's defence for an unspecified period. The US operated Naval Air Station Keflavík from 1951 to 2006, with up to 3,900 troops stationed on Icelandic soil. The base served as a critical anti-submarine warfare and early warning facility during the Cold War, monitoring the GIUK gap (Greenland–Iceland–United Kingdom). Although US forces withdrew in 2006, the bilateral defence agreement remains in force. Iceland is a founding member of NATO (1949) and has no standing military. The 1951 agreement counted Iceland's provision of territory toward its NATO contribution, exempting Iceland from direct financial contributions. US strategic interest in Iceland centres on the North Atlantic's role in maritime surveillance, air policing, and early warning.
source_name: Avalon Project, Yale Law Library — Defense of Iceland: Agreement Between the United States and the Republic of Iceland, May 5, 1951; Government of Iceland — Iceland and the US: Bilateral Relations
caveats: The claim that the US has an interest in keeping Iceland out of the EU is speculative and not supported by any official US policy statement. The US has generally supported European integration. However, EU membership could complicate bilateral defence arrangements through CFSP/CSDP obligations, and some analysts argue the US prefers bilateral relationships with strategically important small allies.

statement: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 significantly increased Iceland's geopolitical importance. NATO allies resumed regular air policing deployments to Keflavík, and in March 2022 the Icelandic government considered whether continuous air policing was needed rather than periodic rotations. The EU adopted the Strategic Compass in March 2022, establishing a framework for EU security and defence policy through 2030, including a 5,000-strong EU Rapid Deployment Capacity. The Strategic Compass explicitly states that EU defence is 'complementary to NATO', but the EU's growing defence ambitions — including a proposed EUR 150 billion European defence spending plan (2025) — create potential overlap with NATO structures. For Iceland, EU membership would add a CSDP layer to its existing NATO-centred security architecture, potentially complicating the bilateral US-Iceland defence relationship and Iceland's unique position as a NATO member without armed forces.
source_name: EU Strategic Compass for Security and Defence (March 2022); NATO Allied Air Command — Icelandic Air Policing; IISS Strategic Survey 2023
caveats: The interaction between EU defence policy and NATO is evolving and uncertain. Twenty-three EU member states are also NATO members, and institutional overlap is managed pragmatically. Iceland's lack of armed forces makes CSDP participation largely symbolic. The claim that EU membership would undermine the US-Iceland bilateral relationship is speculative — many NATO allies are also EU members without conflict.
